coredata.dc:title: The synthesis of water-dispersible zinc doped AgInS2 quantum dots and their application in Cu2+ detection
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yongfeng
Deng, Ming
Zhu, Tao
Tang, Xiaosheng
Han, Shuai
Huang, Wei
Shi, Yali
Liu, Anping
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a facile and effective method based on mini-emulsion/solvent evaporation technique was designed to synthesize water-soluble zinc-doped AgInS2 (AIZS) quantum dots stabilized by dodecyltrimethylammonium bromide (DTAB). Subsequently, the as-synthesized nanocomposites were successfully used as sensitive and selective fluorescence nanoprobe for copper(II) detection. Fluorescence of the cadmium-free AIZS-DTAB QDs could be significantly quenched upon the addition of copper(II), and the quenching degree (I0/I) was proportionally linear with copper(II) concentration ranging from 0.05 to 10μM with a low detection limitation of 15nM. The quenching mechanism was found to be due to electron transfer mechanism as confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements. This nano-probe shows good selectivity toward copper(II) in aqueous solution with a quick response of 1min, offering great potential to determine the copper(II) concentration in drinking water and natural water resources.
coredata.dcterms:subject: AIZS quantum dots
Fluorescence
DTAB
Sensors
Copper(II)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Time response of PL intensity of QDs in the presence of Cu2+
PL dependence of AIZS-DTAB QDs on Cu2+ and selectivity toward Cu2+
PH effect of the as-synthesized nanoparticles

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the Key Research and Development Project of Ministry of Science and Technology under grant 2016YFC0801200, the Natural Science Foundation of China under Grants (No. 61635004, 61475028, 61575190 and 61575033), the Chongqing Outstanding Youth Project under Grant cstc2014jcyjjq40002, the Project of Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (Grant No: cstc2013jcyjA40029) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities under Grant 106112015CDJXY120010.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorbance and PL spectra of AIZS QDs before and after capped by DTAB; the characterizations of AIZS-DTAB QDs: (b) TEM image (inset: size distribution), (c) hydrodynamic sizes in water at 25°C (inset: HRTEM image), and (d) FTIR spectrum.
XPS data from the surface of the AIZS-DTAB nanospheres: the XPS spectrum of (a) S(2p), (b) Ag(3d), (c) In(3d), and (d) Zn(2p).

(a) PH effect on PL intensity of the AIZS-DTAB QDs, (b) time response of AIZS-DTAB QDs in the presence of 50nM Cu2+ ions, (c) Fluorescence spectra of AIZS-DTAB QDs in the presence of different concentration of copper(II) ions (from 1 to 11, the concentrations of copper(II) are 0, 0.05, 0.2, 0.7, 1.5, 3.0, 4.6, 6.6, 8.6, 9.6, 10μM, respectively), and (d) the calibration curve for the PL intensity of the QDs with the function of Cu2+ ions with data extracted from peak PL intensity in Fig. 3c.
XPS data from the surface of the AIZS-DTAB QDs: the XPS spectra of (a) S(2p), (b) Ag 3d, (c) In 3d, (d) Zn 2p, Cu(2p) in the presence of 50μM Cu2+ ions, and (f) Cu(2p) in the absence of Cu2+.

FL response of QDs in the absence (Column 1) and presence (Column 2) of 10μM copper(II), and in the presence of 10μM copper(II) containing a specified cation of the same concentration (Column 3–11). The excitation wavelength was 365nm.
Schematic diagram illustrating the formation of AIZS-DTAB nanocomposites using DTAB.
Schematic illustration for the fluorescence quenching of DTAB-capped AIZS QDs by Cu2+.
Reports of QDs-based fluorescent copper(II) sensor.

head.ce:title: The synthesis of water-dispersible zinc doped AgInS2 quantum dots and their application in Cu2+ detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Technology & Systems of the Education Ministry of China, College of Optoelectronic Engineering, Chongqing University, Chongqing 400044, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Technology & Systems of the Education Ministry of China, College of Optoelectronic Engineering, Chongqing University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Foreign Language, Xihua University, Chengdu 610039, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Foreign Language, Xihua University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Structure and Function, Chongqing University, Chongqing 401331, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Structure and Function, Chongqing University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a facile and effective method based on mini-emulsion/solvent evaporation technique was designed to synthesize water-soluble zinc-doped AgInS2 (AIZS) quantum dots stabilized by dodecyltrimethylammonium bromide (DTAB). Subsequently, the as-synthesized nanocomposites were successfully used as sensitive and selective fluorescence nanoprobe for copper(II) detection. Fluorescence of the cadmium-free AIZS-DTAB QDs could be significantly quenched upon the addition of copper(II), and the quenching degree (I0/I) was proportionally linear with copper(II) concentration ranging from 0.05 to 10μM with a low detection limitation of 15nM. The quenching mechanism was found to be due to electron transfer mechanism as confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements. This nano-probe shows good selectivity toward copper(II) in aqueous solution with a quick response of 1min, offering great potential to determine the copper(II) concentration in drinking water and natural water resources.

ce:section.ce:para: Colloidal semiconductor quantum dots with unique optical properties have generated great research interests in the past several decades. In particular, they are very attractive as fluorescent probes for the detection of heavy metal ions due to their broader absorption spectra, narrower spectral bandwidth and tunable emission wavelength in comparison with organic dyes [1–6]. Copper, one kind of heavy metal ions and essential element for many living organisms, would become toxic at high concentrations. Recently, copper was recognized as the murderer causing the liver damage in infants [7,8]. Therefore, the fluorescent probes based on semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) for selective and sensitive Cu2+ detection has received growing attention in environmental and biological fields [9,10]. Chen and Rosenzweig demonstrated the first example of Cu2+ and Zn2+ analysis by utilizing CdS luminescent QDs capped by different ligands in aqueous media [11]. He and his co-workers reported the ability of applying glyphosate modified CdTe/CdS QDs as a sensitive turn-off probe for Cu2+ [12]. Batteas and his co-workers synthesized the 16-mercaptohexadecanoic acid coated CdSe QDs for optical detection of the Cu(II) based on fluorescence change of the nanoparticles after the addition of copper(II) [13]. However, phase transfer by thiol-derivative molecules into water-dispersible QDs always renders low quantum yield (QY), which exerts detrimental influence on the sensitivity of these developments [14]. In addition, the main concern about the use of these fluorescent probes based on QDs is represented by their inherent toxicity, as most of them are composed of highly toxic elements (e.g. Hg, Cd, Te), which are known to be highly poisonous for living organisms [15–17]. On the other hand, time response of these sensors are relatively long due to the quenching mechanism of chemical displacement by the added heavy metal or the binding between ligand and heavy metals. Therefore, this arouses to a strong interest in searching for alternative luminescent probes that contain no cadmium element and have higher sensitivity with fast response. Hence, it could be advantageous if there is a convenient method assembling these semiconductor nanocrystals with nontoxicity elements into one kind of ligand that endows the QDs good selectivity and high sensitivity toward heavy metal ions. It's known that dodecyltrimethylammonium bromide (DTAB), a kind of cationic surfactants, is apposite to be employed for phase transferring based on its low cost, easy fabrication, and excellent hydrophilic property. To date, cadmium-free fluorescent quantum dots capped by amphiphilic DTAB have not been reported for toxic heavy metal detection.

In this study, we present a facile method to synthesize water-dispersible zinc-doped AgInS2 quantum dots (termed AIZS QDs) modified by DTAB using mini-emulsion/solvent evaporation technique. The nanocomposites have been applied as sensitive probe for Cu2+ ions, while good selectivity toward Cu2+ was achieved, which can be attributed to the good oxidability of Cu2+. In addition, this probe has an ultralow limitation of detection of 15nM arising from its high sensitivity to Cu2+ and good stability of the QDs in blank signals. The photoluminescence (PL) of nanocomposites was significantly quenched in the presence of Cu2+ due to the electron transfer from QDs to the added copper(II), which can be verified by the XPS spectra. The pH effect of the probe and time response to copper (II) were also discussed. This sensor is of great potential in copper(II) detection in drinking water and natural water resources.

Silver nitrate (AgNO3, ≥98%), oleic acid (99.0%), technical grade 1-octadecene (ODE, 90%), indium(III) acetate (In(Ac)3, 99.99%), oleylamine (OA), 1-dodecanethiol (DDT, ≥98%), and technical grade zinc stearate were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Dodecyltrimethylammonium bromide (DTAB, ≥98%), Copper sulfate (CuSO4, 97%), 1M Tris-HCl buffer resolution (pH=7.4), and other reagents were purchased from Shanghai Tansoole Technology Company. The water used in the experiments were deionized (DI) water. All chemical reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification.

Oleic acid capped AIZS QDs were firstly prepared according to our previous report [18]. In order to solubilize the hydrophobic nanoparticles into an aqueous phase, nanoclusters consisting of hydrophobic AIZS QDs encapsulated by DTAB were synthesized for the first time. The nanoclusters were formed by a mini-emulsion/solvent evaporation technique as illustrated in Scheme 1. Briefly, 2mL AIZS QDs in CHCl3 (5mg/mL) were mixed with 5mL DTAB in water (4mg/mL). Under vigorous stirring for about 10min, the mixture was thoroughly emulsified. The DTAB-stabilized AIZS QDs were prepared after removing chloroform by evaporation at 70°C for 20min using a magnetic stirrer. The as-prepared clear solution first underwent centrifugation at 2000rpm for 5min to remove aggregates. It was further purified by passing through a 0.22µm syringe filter, followed by dialyzing in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 500Da) against DI-H2O for 12h to remove the un-reacted small molecules, and finally the AIZS-DTAB QDs in deionized water were stored in enclosed glass vials at room temperature.

In order to obtain optimum experimental conditions, pH effect on the PL intensity of AIZS-DTAB nanocomposites was investigated. Typically, 1mL as-synthesized AIZS-DTAB QDs, HCl, NaOH, and 1mL Tris-HCl buffer solution used to adjust the solution pH value in the range of 5–9. Corresponding PL spectra were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time response of PL intensity of QDs in the presence of Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: The time response of PL intensity of QDs in the presence of 50nM Cu2+ was measured. 1mL as-synthesized AIZS-DTAB QDs, 1mL Tris-HCl buffer solution used to adjust the solution pH value at 7.4 were mixed with some copper(II) stock solution and the mixture was diluted with deionized water into 4mL. Then fluorescence sepectra of QDs at different time were measured in a cuvette at room temperature using Fluorescence Spectrophotometer with excitation wavelength at 365nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL dependence of AIZS-DTAB QDs on Cu2+ and selectivity toward Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: PL dependence of such QDs on Cu2+ and selectivity toward Cu2+ were carried out by just changing the stock solutions, while keeping other conditions unchanged. Typically, 1mL as-synthesized AIZS-DTAB QDs, 1mL Tris-HCl buffer solution used to adjust the solution pH value at 7.4 were mixed with some stock solution. The sensor solutions for the detection of copper(II) were in the mixture of Tris-HCl buffer solution and deionized water except for special statement. Fluorescence were measured in a cuvette at room temperature using Fluorescence Spectrophotometer with excitation wavelength at 365nm after incubating one minute in order to get QDs thoroughly to react with copper(II). The slit widths of excitation and emission are 10nm and 10nm, respectively.

Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS was used to obtain the dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) of the nanoparticles. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured by ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer UV-2450 (SHIMADZU) and Nicolet IS5 FTIR, respectively. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded using fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse G9800A). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with multifuntional imaging electron spectrometer (Thermo ESCALAB 250XI), using an unmonochromated Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6eV) with a source power of 150W. Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) images were performed using thermal field emission transmission electron microscopy (ZEISS LIBRA 200 FEG). Samples characterized in TEM were prepared by drying in an ambient environment after dipping carbon-coated copper grids into the sample solution. All optical measurements were performed at room temperature. PL quantum yield (QY) of the NCs in deionized water was calculated by comparing their integrated emission with that of pure deionized water using fluorescence spectroscopy (FLS-980, Edinburgh).

Water-dispersible AIZS-DTAB QDs were synthesized through oil-in-water miniemulsion and solvent evaporation process. Such formation mechanism is driven by the hydrophobic van der interactions between the hydrocarbon chain of AIZS QDs ligands (i.e., oleic acid) and the hydrocarbon chain of the surfactant (DTAB in this experiment). The characterizations of DTAB-capped AIZS QDs are displayed in Fig. 1. Absorption and PL spectra dispersed in CHCl3 and water are both displayed in Fig. 1a. It can be seen that the absorption spectrum of QDs capped by DTAB hard slightly red shift compared with that dispersed in CHCl3, indicating that the nanoparticles in aqueous solutions are more stable. In comparison with AIZS QDs in CHCl3, there is no wavelength shift in PL intensity of QDs capped by DTAB though a common decrease in PL intensity. On the other hand, the quantum yield of AIZS-DTAB NCs is 31.2%, smaller than that of oil AIZS QDs (39.6%). The reduction in FL intensity and QY of AIZS-DTAB QDs is possibly ascribed to the reaction between the QDs and the DTAB network, which blocks the fluorescence emissions of the QDs. Besides, the QD preparation and surface treatment processes may also contribute to the decrease of PL. The PL spectrum of AIZS-DTAB composites was symmetric, indicating that they were homogeneous and monodispersed. Such spectra indicate that this method effectively preserves the PL property due to the hydrophobic-hydrophobic interaction between the AIZS QDs.

The size, shape, morphology of AIZS-DTAB QDs were also characterized by thermal field emission transmission electron microscopy. The TEM image and typical particle-size distribution show that the QDs were monodispersed and the average diameter of the torispherical QDs is about 4.5nm as displayed in Fig. 1b and its inset. Besides, the hydrodynamic size in water of AIZS-DTAB QDs as plotted in Fig. 1c, recorded by Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS, is 50.05 ± 1.5nm with a narrow distribution, confirming that the nanoparticles are synthesized in uniform. The lattice fringes of the composites were clearly observed by high resolution TEM (HRTEM), as shown in the inset of Fig. 1c, suggesting that the AIZS nanoparticles in aqueous solution were well crystallized. High-resolution TEM image further reveals the crystalline characteristics of the AIZS-DTAB composites, showing that the lattice fringes of individual AIZS clusters, corresponding to 101 plane, were 0.31nm. In order to check the chemical groups of the as-synthesized nanocomposites, FTIR spectrum was also recorded as shown in Fig. 1d. The broadening absorption bands near 3332 and 1636cm−1 are possibly owing to the stretching vibration of –OH originated from intercalated water. The two significantly weaker bands at 2925 and 2855cm−1 are due to the stretch vibrations of the –CH3 terminal group of the surfactant hydrocarbon tail [19,20]. The absorption band occurring near 1490cm−1 arises from the stretch vibrations of –CH2 [21]. This FTIR spectrum indicates that the AIZS QDs was successfully modified by DTAB. All the characterizations displayed above show that AIZS-DTAB QDs dispersed in water were synthesized through correct method.

In order to analyze the surface chemical states, element composites, and the valence of the nanoparticles, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was required to analyze AIZS-DTAB nanocomposites. From Fig. 2a, it could be realized that the S(2p) peaks were 161.3 and 162.2eV. As shown in Fig. 2b, the binding energies for Ag(3d5/2) and Ag (3d3/2) were 367.0 and 373.5eV. It was observed that the In(3d) core split into 3d5/2 (444.5eV) and 3d3/2 (452.5eV) peaks (Fig. 2c). The XPS spectrum of Zn(2p) was showed in Fig. 3d, the peaks for Zn(2p3/2) and Zn(2p1/2) were 1022 and 1044eV. All of the above measurement results showed that the valence of the elements S, Ag, In, and Zn were 2-, 1+, 3+, and 2+, respectively, which were in agreement with the previous reports [22,23], indicating that the nanomaterials have been successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PH effect of the as-synthesized nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The pH effect in the range from 5.0 to 9.0 on the PL intensity of the QDs was investigated in order to seek out the optimal conditions for copper(II) ion determination. The as-prepared quantum dots were added into a 10mL flask, following by adding Tris, HCl (36.7%) and deionized water to adjust the pH value from 5.0 to 9.0 and to dilute into 4mL and 0.5mg/mL concentration of AIZS-DTAB QDs. The PL intensities were measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer as shown in Fig. 3a. We can see that the PL displayed a trend of first ascent and then descent with the maximum fluorescence intensity at pH 7.4. The low fluorescence intensity under acid conditions may be due to protonation of the surface amino group which causes some defects of the DTAB capping layer, and favors the approach of quencher to the QDs core [24]. The maximum fluorescence intensity of the QDs occurs with pH value at 7.4 owing to its deprotonation effect. It has been shown that high QDs concentration leads to larger sensitivity, whereas too high concentration results in low sensitivity. Therefore, probe solution of 0.5mg/mL with pH value at 7.4 was adopted to obtain the desired fluorescence in the following experiments.

Response time of AIZS-DTAB QDs to Cu2+ was investigated as shown in Fig. 3b. It can be seen that the PL intensity of the probe in the presence of 50nM Cu2+ was gradually quenched and then kept nearly stable within one minute, which is indicative that electron transfer from nanoparticles to the added Cu2+ is a fast process. Fast response time of 1min, much faster than the reported ones [7,25], makes the QDs quick-detection sensors for Cu2+ in aqueous water. Besides, the adding sequences were also investigated, and the adding sequence of QDS, Tris-HCl buffer solution, and copper(II) was found to lead to stronger and steadier quench fluorescence signal.

The typical fluorescence spectra as functions of Cu2+ was shown in Fig. 3c. As can be seen from the PL spectra, the emission peak intensities decreased gradually with the increase of Cu2+ ion concentration, until no further decrease in PL intensity was observed when Cu2+ concentration was larger than 10μM, suggesting that no additional copper ion could be bound to the QD surface, which was triggered by the nanoparticles becoming saturated. Meanwhile, the spectral widths and emission peak wavelengths of the QDs remained unchanged, an indication of unaffected band gap of nanoparticles.

The relationship between I0/I at 601nm and Cu2+ concentration is well revealed in Fig. 3d with the measured data extracted from the emission peak with different concentration of Cu2+. The PL dependence on the copper(II) concentration can be well described by the following Stern-Volmer equation:
I
0
/ I = 1 + K
SV
[ Q ]
where I0 and I are the fluorescence intensity of the probe solution in the absence and presence of a given concentration of copper(II), respectively. Herein, KSV, the Stern-Volmer constant (slope), was found to be 0.18*106 M−1 in the present study, while [Q] was the concentration of added copper(II). The sensor possessed a linear range of 0.05 – 10μM between I0/I and Cu2+ concentration. At the same time, the R2 of 0.998 and a low LOD of 15nM were achieved, defined by 3σ/KSV, where the σ and KSV standed for standard deviation of blank signal (n=8) and the sensitivity of the calibration curve (slope), which was much smaller than the ones reported in Table 1 [12,26–36]. Table 1 compared the detection performance of the Cu2+ sensor proposed in this study with various existing optical based metal ion sensors. Note that the table showed not only the detection limit of each sensor, but also the relevant surface stabilizers. It could be seen that the QDs modified with different organic ligands also had fluorescence response to Cu2+ with different LOD. Compared with QDs stabilized by different ligands, the DTAB-coated AIZS QDs possessed lower LOD.

Zhu et al. reported the use of L-cysteine-capped CdSeTe alloyed quantum dots for the ultrasensitive detection of Cu2+, whose quenching fluorescence was attributed to the competitive binding of the cysteine with Cu2+, leading to the loss of cysteine, and subsequently causing aggragation and precipitation of the QDs with obvious red shift in emission wavelength [37]. However, the quenching fluorescence of AIZS-DTAB QDs by Cu2+ was possibly different due to no appearance of wavelength shift in the whole experiment. Therefore, XPS measurements were performed in an effort to figure out the quenching mechanism of AIZS-DTAB QDs in the presence of Cu2+. Fig. 4a-e show the XPS spectra of AIZS-DTAB QDs after reacting with 50μM Cu2+. In comparison with XPS spectra of the nanoparticles without presence of Cu2+, it could be observed that the XPS spectra of the AIZS-DTAB QDs in the presence of 50μM Cu2+ exhibit no significant change in binding energies of S(2p), Ag(3d), In(3d), and Zn(2p) (Fig. 4a-d), which is indicative that the added Cu2+ exert almost no significant effect on the band gap of the QDs. High-resolution photoelectron spectra of the Cu(2p) of the QDs in the presence of 50μM Cu2+ ions shows two peaks centering at 931.3 ± 0.1 and 951.2 ± 0.1eV, corresponding to the Cu(2p3/2) and Cu(2p1/2), respectively, which are the characteristics of Cu+ [38], whereas no signal of Cu(2p) was observed for AIZS-DTAB QDs without the presence of Cu2+ ions as displayed in Fig. 4f. Therefore, it could be concluded that the PL quenching mechanism might be ascribed to the electron transfer from the ligand to the added Cu2+ ions, which leads to the Cu2+ reduction into Cu+, facilitating nonradiative recombination of excited electrons (e-) in the conduction band and holes (h+) in the valence band. Scheme 2 gave a clear illustration for the fluorescence quenching of DTAB-capped AIZS QDs by Cu2+ [39]. Upon photonexcitation, electrons transit from valency band (VB) to conduction band (CB), leaving holes in the VB, followed by relaxation to the trap/defect states [40]. In the presence of Cu2+ ions, electron from trap states effectively transfer to Cu2+ ions, quenching the PL of such NCs to some degree.

The interferences of other cations, such as Ag+, Cd2+,Fe3+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, on copper(II) detection were also investigated as shown in Fig. 5. It can be obviously seen that there is no significant change in the emission intensity of QDs in the coexistence of copper(II) and other metal ions, while Fe3+ ions exert small interference on the sensor, which interference can be possibly eliminated by adding fluoride ion to the solution in the form of FeF6 3- [11]. This selectivity, resulting from the electrode potential of Cu2+/Cu+ and good oxidability of Cu2+, made Cu2+ most appropriate receptors for the transferred electron from QDs, leading to the effective fluorescent quenching of the QDs. The selectivity toward copper(II) makes the solution sensor have great promise for practical applications.

In summary, we have proposed a facile method to transfer the oil-dispersible AIZS QDs into water-dispersible ones using amphiphilic DTAB as stabilizing agent through a mini-emulsion/solvent evaporation technique. Such hydrophilic NCs possessed high QY (up to 31.2%). The as-prepared AIZS-DTAB QDs dispersed in aqueous solutions were successfully applied for sensitive copper(II) determination in aqueous solution. Besides, the pH value effect on the PL intensity of the QDs was investigated to select an optimal condition for copper(II) determination with the help of Tris-HCl buffer solution to adjust the solution pH value. It was found that the probe solution at pH 7.4 obtained the maximum emission intensity. The PL intensity of nanoparticles were quenched upon the addition of copper(II). Such quenching mechanism of the electron transfer to copper(II) led to the reduction of Cu2+ to Cu+, which has been verified by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. In addition, the PL dependence on copper(II) concentration can be well described by the Stern-Volmer equation with a high sensitivity of 0.18*106 M−1. Moreover, the cadmium-free nanoprobe, possessing good selectivity toward Cu2+ with a LOD as low as 15nM and a short time response of 1min, offers great potentials in copper(II) detection in drinking water and natural water resources.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Key Research and Development Project of Ministry of Science and Technology under grant 2016YFC0801200, the Natural Science Foundation of China under Grants (No. 61635004, 61475028, 61575190 and 61575033), the Chongqing Outstanding Youth Project under Grant cstc2014jcyjjq40002, the Project of Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (Grant No: cstc2013jcyjA40029) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities under Grant 106112015CDJXY120010.
